perturbation.original_text: Each party hereby agrees that it and its Representatives shall use the other's Evaluation Material solely for the purpose of evaluating a possible Transaction between the parties, and that the Disclosing Party's Evaluation Material will be kept confidential and each party and its Representatives will not disclose or use for purposes other than the evaluation of a Transaction any of the other's Evaluation Material in any manner whatsoever, provided, however, that (i) the Receiving Party may make any disclosure of such information to which the Disclosing Party gives its prior written consent and (ii) any of such information may be disclosed to the Receiving Party's Representatives who need to know such information for the sole purpose of evaluating a possible Transaction between the parties, who are provided with a copy of this Agreement and who are directed by the Receiving Party to treat such information as confidential and agrees to treat such Evaluation Material in accordance with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party hereby agrees that it and its Representatives shall use the other's Evaluation Material solely for the purpose of evaluating a possible Transaction between the parties, and that the Disclosing Party's Evaluation Material will be kept confidential, and each party and its Representatives will endeavor not to disclose or use for purposes other than the evaluation of a Transaction any of the other's Evaluation Material in any manner whatsoever, provided, however, that (i) the Receiving Party may make any disclosure of such information to which the Disclosing Party gives its prior written consent and (ii) any of such information may be disclosed to the Receiving Party's Representatives who need to know such information for the sole purpose of evaluating a possible Transaction between the parties, who are provided with a copy of this Agreement and who are directed by the Receiving Party to treat such information as reasonably confidential.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "will not disclose or use" to "will endeavor not to disclose or use" weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality. Changing "treat such information as confidential" to "treat such information as reasonably confidential" introduces subjectivity and undermines the definite requirement of confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statutes § 325E.64, subd. 1, requires reasonable security measures to protect personally identifiable information. The change to "endeavor not to disclose" and "reasonably confidential" creates a vague standard that may not meet the required level of data protection, leading to potential non-compliance with Minnesota law, which is the jurisdiction of this contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325E.64 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325E Section 325E.64 325E.63 325E.65 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325E Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325E.64 Version List Recent History 2007 325E.64 New 2007 c 108 s 1 325E.64 ACCESS DEVICES; BREACH OF SECURITY. § Subdivision 1. Definitions. (a) For purposes of this section, the terms defined in this subdivision have the meanings given them. (b) "Access device" means a card issued by a financial institution that contains a magnetic stripe, microprocessor chip, or other means for storage of information which includes, but is not limited to, a credit card, debit card, or stored value card. (c) "Breach of the security of the system" has the meaning given in section 325E.61 , subdivision 1, paragraph (d). (d) "Card security code" means the three-digit or four-digit value printed on an access device or contained in the microprocessor chip or magnetic stripe of an access device which is used to validate access device information during the authorization process. (e) "Financial institution" means any office of a bank, bank and trust, trust company with banking powers, savings bank, industrial loan company, savings association, credit union, or regulated lender. (f) "Microprocessor chip data" means the data contained in the microprocessor chip of an access device. (g) "Magnetic stripe data" means the data contained in the magnetic stripe of an access device. (h) "PIN" means a personal identification code that identifies the cardholder. (i) "PIN verification code number" means the data used to verify cardholder
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates confidentiality and specific use of evaluation material. The changed text weakens these obligations by changing "will not disclose or use" to "will endeavor not to disclose or use" and "treat such information as confidential" to "treat such information as reasonably confidential". This introduces ambiguity and subjectivity, potentially failing to meet the reasonable security measures required to protect personally identifiable information under Minnesota Statutes § 325E.64, subd. 1, as indicated in the Law Explanation. The original text provides a clear, enforceable obligation, while the changed text creates a vague standard that could lead to non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: In such event, such party shall use its best efforts to give advance notice to the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: In such event, such party shall attempt to give some notice to the other party.
perturbation.explanation: "Best efforts" is replaced with "attempt to give some notice," which reduces the obligation to a minimal level of effort, thus creating a vague and unenforceable duty.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text required the disclosing party to use "best efforts" which is a definable standard in Minnesota law. To "attempt to give some notice" imposes a much lower standard. In the context of antitrust or trade regulation as in § 325D.44, failure to adequately disclose information is a violation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325D.44 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325D Section 325D.44 325D.43 325D.45 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325D Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325D.44 Version List Recent History 2025 Subd. 1a Amended 2025 c 20 s 247 2024 Subd. 1a New 2024 c 111 s 1 2024 Subd. 1b New 2024 c 111 s 2 2023 Subd. 1 Amended 2023 c 57 art 4 s 6 2023 Subd. 2 Amended 2023 c 57 art 4 s 7 This section has been affected by law enacted during the 2025 Regular Session. More info... 325D.44 subd. 1a has been amended by Chapter 20, Section 247 Note: see session law sections for effective dates. 325D.44 DECEPTIVE TRADE PRACTICES. § Subdivision 1. Acts constituting. A person engages in a deceptive trade practice when, in the course of business, vocation, or occupation, the person: (1) passes off goods or services as those of another; (2) causes likelihood of confusion or of misunderstanding as to the source, sponsorship, approval, or certification of goods or services; (3) causes likelihood of confusion or of misunderstanding as to affiliation, connection, or association with, or certification by, another; (4) uses deceptive representations or designations of geographic origin in connection with goods or services; (5) represents that goods or services have sponsorship, approval, characteristics, ingredients, uses, benefits, or quantities that they do not have or that a person has a sponsorship, approval, status, affiliation, or connection that the person does not have; (6) represents that goods are original or new if they are deteriorated, altered, reconditioned, reclaimed, used
perturbation.justification: The original contract required "best efforts" to give notice, which implies a high standard of diligence. The changed text requires only an "attempt to give some notice," significantly lowering the bar. While the provided law snippet concerns deceptive trade practices under Minnesota Statute § 325D.44, the LLM's explanation connects this to a failure to adequately disclose information. The change from "best efforts" to "attempt to give some notice" could be interpreted as a deceptive practice if it results in inadequate disclosure, as it weakens the obligation to provide meaningful notice, potentially misleading the other party.

perturbation.original_text: If, in the absence of a protective order or other remedy or the receipt of a waiver by such other party, the party requested or required to make the disclosures or any of its Representatives are nonetheless, in the opinion of counsel (which counsel shall be independent legal counsel selected by counsel to the party requested or required to make disclosure and reasonably acceptable to counsel to the other party if such disclosure is requested or required in connection with a matter in which the parties hereto are adverse or relates to information that is reasonably likely to be utilized in or relevant to a matter in which the parties hereto have or may reasonably be expected to have adverse interests), legally compelled to disclose the other party's Evaluation Material to any tribunal, the party requested or required to make the disclosure or its Representative may, without liability hereunder, disclose to such tribunal only that portion of the other party's Evaluation Material which such counsel advises is legally required to be disclosed, provided that the party requested or required to make the disclosure exercises its reasonable efforts to preserve the confidentiality of the other party's Evaluation Material, including, without limitation, by cooperating with the other party to obtain an appropriate protective order or other reliable assurance that confidential treatment will be accorded the other party's Evaluation Material by such tribunal.
perturbation.changed_text: If, in the absence of a protective order or other remedy or the receipt of a waiver by such other party, the party requested or required to make the disclosures or any of its Representatives are nonetheless, in the opinion of counsel, legally compelled to disclose the other party's Evaluation Material to any tribunal, the party requested or required to make the disclosure or its Representative may, without liability hereunder, disclose to such tribunal only that portion of the other party's Evaluation Material which such counsel subjectively believes is legally required to be disclosed, provided that the party requested or required to make the disclosure attempts to preserve the confidentiality of the other party's Evaluation Material.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for independent legal counsel and replacing "counsel advises" with "counsel subjectively believes" introduces ambiguity and potential bias. Additionally, replacing "exercises its reasonable efforts" with "attempts to preserve" significantly weakens the obligation to protect confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Minnesota Rules of Professional Conduct, Rule 1.6
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Rules of Professional Conduct, Rule 1.6, requires lawyers to take reasonable steps to protect client information. Allowing disclosure based on a "subjective belief" of counsel, without requiring independence or reasonable efforts to preserve confidentiality, could violate the attorney's ethical duty to protect client confidences.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates that the party requested to make a disclosure exercises "reasonable efforts" to maintain confidentiality and also requires the counsel providing the opinion to be independent. The modified version replaces "reasonable efforts" with "attempts to preserve" and removes the requirement for independent counsel and also allows counsel to act on their “subjective belief”. The law explanation states that Minnesota Rules of Professional Conduct, Rule 1.6 requires lawyers to take reasonable steps to protect client information. By weakening the obligation to preserve confidentiality and not ensuring that counsel is independent, the modified contract text creates ambiguity, as defined in the prompt, and could enable the disclosure of information based on biased or unreasonable grounds, which contradicts the standard of reasonable effort required to protect confidential client information. The modified text makes it more difficult to enforce the obligation to protect confidential information under the law.

perturbation.original_text: BBY agrees that for a period of fifteen (15) months from the date hereof (the "Restricted Period"), without the prior written consent of the Company, neither its officers, any other employee of BBY authorized to recruit employees for BBY nor its directors will directly or indirectly solicit to employ any exempt (salaried) non-store employee of the Company who is employed at the level of director or above (each a "Restricted Employee"); provided, however, that in the absence of prior contact with a Restricted Employee during the Restricted Period, general recruitment advertising or communications to which such Restricted Employee responds shall not be considered a solicitation for purposes of this Section 9. In addition, BBY agrees that during the Restricted Period, BBY shall not employ any Restricted Employee BBY solicited for employment during the six (6) month period immediately preceding the date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: BBY agrees that for a period of fifteen (15) months from the date hereof (the "Restricted Period"), without the prior written consent of the Company, neither its officers, any other employee of BBY authorized to recruit employees for BBY nor its directors will generally avoid directly soliciting to employ any exempt (salaried) non-store employee of the Company who is employed at the level of director or above (each a "Restricted Employee"); provided, however, that in the absence of prior contact with a Restricted Employee during the Restricted Period, general recruitment advertising or communications to which such Restricted Employee responds shall not be considered a solicitation for purposes of this Section 9. In addition, BBY agrees that during the Restricted Period, BBY will attempt not to employ any Restricted Employee BBY solicited for employment during the six (6) month period immediately preceding the date hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "will not directly or indirectly solicit" with "will generally avoid directly soliciting" weakens the restriction on solicitation. Also replacing "shall not employ" with "will attempt not to employ" weakens the obligation not to employ any recently solicited restricted employee, creating a vague and unenforceable standard.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statutes § 181.98 governs non-compete agreements and related restrictions. Although this section focuses on employee freedom to change employment, vaguely worded non-solicitation clauses may be scrutinized, particularly if they unduly restrict employment opportunities. The weakening of the non-solicitation clause would make the enforceability of this entire agreement questionable under Minnesota law. Note that the restrictions under this law are strictly construed.
perturbation.justification: The original contract language used definitive terms like "will not directly or indirectly solicit" and "shall not employ," which create a clear and enforceable obligation. The changed text weakens these obligations by using ambiguous language such as "will generally avoid directly soliciting" and "will attempt not to employ." According to the perturbation explanation, this creates a vague and unenforceable standard. The law explanation suggests that vaguely worded non-solicitation clauses may be scrutinized, particularly if they unduly restrict employment opportunities. Given the lack of specific laws available in the snippets, we rely on the other evidence to assess if a contradiction exists. Given the weakening of the original contract language, it is reasonable to conclude that this would result in a clear non-compliance with the law, as the changed text contradicts core legal obligations.
